next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

Blue, salted morning leaked through the blinds and across the meeting folder on Leela's desk while the radiator hammered the pipe behind it. The noise had started at six and never settled back into mere noise. She had given up on sleep, shoved the blanket aside, and crossed the studio barefoot, the floorboards cold enough to bite. Her right hand had curled during the night. She pulled the wrist brace tighter with her left, listening to the Velcro rasp in the dark.

The folder held exactly what she had left there: dissertation abstract, chapter summaries, projected timeline, the one-page defense calendar she had built and rebuilt until every week between March and June looked occupied by progress instead of panic. She had printed the calendar on better paper than the rest because the department copier made ordinary sheets feel temporary. If today went the way it was supposed to go, she would email the graduate coordinator by four, hold a room for late April, and stop opening the graduate-housing portal just to stare at the line about summer vacancy. If today went well, the credit-card minimum on the eighteenth would remain what it had been for months, a nuisance rather than a referendum. If today went well, the money her mother had sent in November could stay filed under bridge instead of beginning to look like subsidy.

She boiled water for coffee she did not really want, then drank it because her body no longer recognized the difference between ritual and appetite. The mug warmed her palm through the chipped glaze. She ate half a banana gone soft at the stem...
open_hooks_to_carry: Treat the forwarded email as social violence rather than gadget-plot surprise.
Show Mohit's counterattack as elegant, plausible, and reputationally savvy.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...Leela attached the scanned notes, the deck screenshots, Omar's statement, the funding-letter trail, the chronology, the cover memo. She addressed the email to Gloria Fisk and the graduate studies grievance address, then copied the ombuds office because June insisted on one witness outside the department and Omar said witnesses mattered more than hope. The subject line looked flimsy no matter how she phrased it: Graduate grievance regarding supervisory misconduct and misuse of student research. She re-read it until the words flattened.

"Send it," June said.

Leela clicked.

For a moment nothing happened. Then the message vanished into Sent. The auto-response arrived fifty-three seconds later, chilled and neutral, thanking them for their submission and promising review according to university procedure. Dawn had begun somewhere outside the basement windows too high to see from here. The fluorescent lights still made everybody look sick.

Leela shut the laptop. Server hum filled the room, along with the cheap wall clock clicking toward six.

She slid the envelope back into the inner sleeve of her backpack.

The gum stopped moving in June's jaw as Leela folded the flap shut, making the decisive pages small enough to disappear again. The complaint now existed in three inboxes and at least two offices. It could hurt them. It could maybe start something. No relief reached June's face. What settled there instead was colder: the knowledge that the version they had just sent was the weakened one, because Leela had wanted a version of justice that still left her something to defend.
state_deltas_required: After Ben forwards the grievance to Mohit, the students need to keep Mohit from defining the story first.
June takes the meeting and withdraws, Ben becomes both ashamed and more entangled, and the alliance shatters at the worst possible moment.
